record.text: here's what i wrote

images.alt:
More so than most of his predecessors, Trump strained against the limits of the presidency. He never understood that the office was bound by higher law — that his power wasn’t absolute. He never understood that he was an officer of the Constitution and a servant of the people; he never understood that he was a subject and not the sovereign.

It was because of this fundamental misunderstanding — itself tied to his bottomless solipsism — that Trump tried to twist and turn the presidency into an extension of his ego. To the extent he failed to accomplish this, it was only because he was stymied by those around him — officials who chose to honor their commitment to the Constitution over the interests of one man. Those same officials now warn that if he is given another term in office, he will rule as a tyrant.

America got lucky. It won’t get lucky again. Free of the guardrails that kept him in place the first time, affirmed by the Supreme Court and backed by allies and apparatchiks in the conservative movement, Trump will merge the office of the presidency with himself. He will shake it from its moorings in the Constitution and rebuild it as an instrument of his will, wielded for his friends and against his enemies. In doing so, he will erode the democratic assumptions that undergird our current constitutional order. And he will have the total loyalty of a Republican Party that itself is twisting and abusing the counter-majoritarian features of the American system to undermine and unravel democracy in the states it controls.

value.text: - @jamellebouie.net’s column not long before his reelection on how a second Trump era would likely mean the end of the American republic as we knew it (he didn’t mean 🇺🇸 literally wouldn’t exist; the U.S. has gone thru multiple republics already) has rung in my brain for the past year and a half
Good, necessary... also blindingly obvious and a bit belated. God I hope Bouie writing it for NYT can help get it through the thick skulls of the oldest, most powerful Democrats though.

images.alt:
You can think of this Trump administration as a similar state of affairs. The American people broke something when they gave Trump a second chance in office. And there is no going back to the Union as it was. If Democrats hope to lead the nation to any kind of recovery, much less renewal, they must understand and internalize this fact of the matter.

Broken eggs cannot be mended. To try to do so, to try to return to some notion of normality is to court failure. Worse, it is to play a repeat of the last Democratic administration when, in pursuit of the familiar, the Democratic Party all but passed the baton back to reactionaries working toward something revolutionary.

images.alt: A middle aged man with messy hair is sitting at a cluttered desk with files and folders and a nearly full ashtray with one lit cigarette burning inside it in a dark room,his fingers poised on several keys as if typing. The screen displays readable text along with a small image of a woman on a format that makes the screen look like a digital identification and background check. The scene evokes the notion of a man obsessed with his work and you can see the visible traces of it, a small scar on his right cheek. Worry lines and wrinkles on his face despite his age.  -- So obviously, "messy hair" was a mistake, as was the whole issue with the lit cigarette. Seeming too, a scar, can also mean large gaping wound. >< lol Keep in mind, this too is taking into consideration that I'm using free tools which have no controls, so even though i said specifically that the text should be coherent and legible, it obviously isn't. Bad tools force us to get very creative in order to get half way decent results.
